step_by_step_thinking:
The stages of neurocysticercosis progress through varying degrees of inflammation and edema. Initially, when the cyst is viable and newly formed, there is minimal pericystic inflammation. As the immune response intensifies, the surrounding inflammation grows, causing perilesional edema. Over time, as the cyst undergoes shrinkage and calcification occurs, inflammation decreases and the edema subsides. The final stage—calcified nodular—is characterized by absence of active inflammation, thus no edema remains. The earlier stages involving vesicles and vesicular-colloidal forms show active inflammatory responses with accompanying perilesional edema. Therefore, the stage without edema corresponds to the fully calcified phase where biological activity ceases and healing completes.
\boxed{D} Correct Answer: D — Nodular calcified stage has no edema because by this point, the cyst has undergone full degradation and calcification, eliminating active inflammation and subsequent edema. Early stages (vesicular/colloidal/granular) involve active immune response and edema development. Only in the terminal calcified state does inflammation cease completely, making edema absent.
**Clinical Reasoning:**
Early stages of neurocysticercosis (vesicular, vesicular-colloidal, granular nodular) involve active host immune response leading to perilesional inflammation and edema. As the cyst evolves toward calcification, inflammation diminishes. By the nodule-to-calcified stage, the cyst is inert and no longer triggers inflammatory processes, hence no edema persists.
